audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 1 draft aligned to Hannah, Elkanah, Peninnah, Shiloh prayer, vow, Samuel's birth, weaning, and dedication narrative; later source/witness audit required.
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Hebrew-first naming, restored/mystical separation, narrative ambiguity, institutional power, vulnerable persons, violence, and misuse risks.

verses.restored: Now there was one man from Ramathaim-zophim, from hill country of Ephraim, and his name was Elkanah son of Jeroham, son of Elihu, son of Tohu, son of Zuph, an Ephrathite.
He had two wives. Name of one was Hannah, and name of second Peninnah. Peninnah had children, but Hannah had no children.
That man went up from his city from days to days to bow down and sacrifice to YHWH of armies at Shiloh. There the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were priests to YHWH.
When day came and Elkanah sacrificed, he gave portions to Peninnah his wife and to all her sons and daughters.
But to Hannah he gave one double/choice portion, because he loved Hannah, though YHWH had closed her womb.
Her rival provoked her intensely, to make her thunder/tremble, because YHWH had closed around her womb.
So it happened year by year: as often as she went up to house of YHWH, the other provoked her. Hannah wept and would not eat.
Elkanah her husband said to her, Hannah, why do you weep, and why do you not eat, and why is your heart hurt? Am I not better to you than ten sons?
Hannah rose after eating in Shiloh and after drinking. Eli the priest was sitting upon seat by doorpost of temple of YHWH.
She was bitter of soul, and she prayed to YHWH and wept greatly.

She vowed a vow and said, YHWH of armies, if seeing, you will see affliction of your servant and remember me and not forget your servant, and give to your servant male offspring, then I will give him to YHWH all days of his life, and a razor shall not go up upon his head.
As she multiplied praying before YHWH, Eli was watching her mouth.
Hannah was speaking upon her heart; only her lips moved, but her voice was not heard. Eli considered her intoxicated.
Eli said to her, How long will you make yourself drunk? Remove your wine from upon you.
Hannah answered and said, No, my lord. A woman hard-pressed in spirit am I. Wine and strong drink I have not drunk, but I have poured out my soul before YHWH.
Do not give your servant as a daughter of worthlessness, because from abundance of my complaint and my provocation I have spoken until now.
Eli answered and said, Go in peace, and may God of Israel grant your petition that you asked from him.
She said, May your servant find favor in your eyes. The woman went on her way, ate, and her face was no longer as before.
They rose early in morning, bowed down before YHWH, returned, and came to their house at Ramah. Elkanah knew Hannah his wife, and YHWH remembered her.
It happened at turning of days that Hannah conceived and bore a son. She called his name Samuel, for she said, From YHWH I asked him.
The man Elkanah and all his house went up to sacrifice to YHWH the sacrifice of days and his vow.
But Hannah did not go up, for she said to her husband, Until the boy is weaned; then I will bring him, and he will appear before face of YHWH and remain there perpetually.
Elkanah her husband said to her, Do what is good in your eyes. Remain until you have weaned him; only may YHWH establish his word. So the woman remained and nursed her son until she weaned him.
When she had weaned him, she brought him up with her, with three bulls, one ephah of flour, and a skin of wine. She brought him to house of YHWH at Shiloh, and the boy was young.
They slaughtered the bull and brought the boy to Eli.
She said, Please, my lord, as your soul lives, my lord, I am the woman who stood beside you here, praying to YHWH.
For this boy I prayed, and YHWH gave me my petition that I asked from him.
So I, in turn, have lent/dedicated him to YHWH. All days that he lives, he is one lent/dedicated to YHWH. And he bowed down there to YHWH.

translation_notes: Elkanah's family is called Ephrathite/Ephraimite in a genealogically and geographically difficult phrase; later audit should compare the Ramathaim, Zuph, and Ephrathite traditions.

Hannah's childlessness is never a moral failure, punishment formula, deficiency of womanhood, or evidence of lesser faith. The narrator's statement that YHWH closed her womb belongs to ancient theological narration and must not be weaponized against infertile or childless people.

Peninnah's provocation is harmful, but the text also places both women inside a polygynous household structured by unequal reproductive status and male distribution. Do not turn Peninnah into a misogynistic stereotype or use her motherhood against Hannah.
Elkanah loves Hannah and offers a special portion, yet his question in verse 8 cannot define what should be enough for her or dismiss grief with marital affection.
Hannah's refusal of food arises from distress and harassment. It is not a devotional fasting prescription, eating-disorder model, or proof that suffering makes prayer more effective.
Eli misreads silent prayer as drunkenness. Spiritual authorities must listen before labeling distressed people, and later correction does not erase the initial harm.
'Pouring out my soul' validates embodied lament, anger, complaint, and tears as prayer.

Hannah's vow is made under severe distress within an ancient household and sanctuary system. It must not justify parents dedicating children without consent, religious institutions claiming children, coercive vows, or bargaining away another person's future.
The no-razor clause evokes Nazirite traditions but is textually distinct from a complete Numbers 6 vow; later audit should compare witnesses and reception.
Elkanah 'knows' Hannah in a standard sexual idiom. The verse does not disclose consent language, though modern interpretation must never assume marriage eliminates the need for consent.
YHWH 'remembering' Hannah describes narrative attention, not prior forgetfulness and not a guarantee that prayer produces conception.
Samuel's name explanation is popular wordplay with sha'al, 'ask,' though the name itself is usually related to 'name of God' or 'heard by God.'
Weaning age and the textual variants in sacrifice animals require audit. The child's young age intensifies the ethical caution around dedication and separation.
The verb in verse 28 can mean lend, grant, or dedicate. Samuel remains a person, not property transferred between adults.
Hannah's agency is central: she prays, answers Eli, determines timing of weaning, accompanies Samuel, identifies herself, and interprets the dedication.
safety_note: Do not shame infertility, childlessness, grief, polygyny survivors, or eating under distress. Do not portray Peninnah as proof that women are inherently rivals. Do not use Hannah's vow to justify child dedication without consent, religious ownership of children, coercive parenting, or promises that prayer guarantees pregnancy. Preserve Hannah's lament, agency, and timing.
